title: Rabbit dental problems
subtitle: Why dental disease is so common in domestic rabbits, what the early signs look like, and what diet prevents it.
excerpt: Rabbit teeth grow continuously throughout life and depend on abrasion from fibrous food to wear at a rate that matches growth. A diet heavy in pellets and low in hay fails to provide adequate tooth wear, and the result is malocclusion, overgrown spurs, and root elongation that causes pain, weight loss, and eventually a rabbit that cannot eat. Dental disease is largely a diet problem.
sourceNotes: Harcourt-Brown (2002): Textbook of Rabbit Medicine, Butterworth-Heinemann: dental disease chapter. RSPCA: rabbit nutrition and dental health. Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.): dentistry section.

glossaryTerms.definition: Misalignment of the upper and lower teeth in a rabbit such that they do not occlude (meet) correctly during chewing. In the incisors, malocclusion is visible: the lower incisors protrude in front of the upper. In the cheek teeth, malocclusion is not visible without sedation and examination with an otoscope or dental probe. Cheek tooth malocclusion is far more common and serious than incisor malocclusion.

A sharp overgrowth on the edge of a molar or premolar in a rabbit, caused by uneven wear. Spurs typically develop on the outer edge of the upper cheek teeth and the inner edge of the lower cheek teeth. They cut into the tongue or cheek, causing pain, hypersalivation (wet dewlap), and reluctance to eat.

The proportion of a rabbit's diet that should consist of grass hay. The RWAF and RSPCA both recommend hay constitutes at least 80% of a rabbit's diet by volume. Long-stem grass hay provides the horizontal grinding chewing motion that wears the cheek teeth; pellets do not.

content.text: Dental disease in rabbits develops gradually and is rarely obvious until it is advanced. A rabbit with

cutting into its tongue has been in pain for weeks or months before the signs become apparent to the owner. The best management is dietary prevention and regular health checks that catch the early signs.
Wet fur under the chin or on the chest (hypersalivation from mouth pain).
Selective eating: the rabbit picks up hay and drops it, or prefers softer pellets and vegetables over hay.
Gradual weight loss in an animal that appears to be eating normally.
Facial swelling below the eye or along the jaw, which may indicate a dental root abscess.
Discharge from one or both eyes, which can indicate pressure on the nasolacrimal duct from elongated tooth roots.

principle: at least 80% of the diet by volume should be good quality grass hay. Timothy hay, meadow hay, and orchard grass all provide adequate abrasion. Pellets in a diet-fed rabbit should be limited to a small tablespoon per kilogram of body weight; unlimited pellets from a young age are associated with early dental disease in multiple studies. Fresh leafy greens contribute to dental wear less than long-stem hay but are a useful supplement.

is established in the cheek teeth, it cannot be reversed through diet. A vet can burr down sharp spurs under anaesthesia to restore a chewing surface. Some rabbits need this every 6 to 12 weeks for the rest of their life. X-rays of the skull help assess root damage and the outlook: a rabbit with severe changes may reach a point where further treatment cannot maintain quality of life.
